old_claim.note: Construction supports manufacturing, deployment, commercialization, or operational scaling.

new_claim.note: The trireme is defined by 170 manually worked oars arranged on three levels; sail power supplemented passages but did not replace its constitutive oar system.

source_shape.source_locator: Single-page poster, opening paragraph and sea-trials section: defines the trireme by 170 oars at three levels and reports Olympias operating under both oar and sail.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The Trireme Trust reconstruction summary identifies the three-level, 170-oar arrangement as the vessel's defining propulsion architecture.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Manual boat propulsion names the actual motive system, whereas the removed construction node is scoped to monumental stone and civic works and contributes no ship-specific mechanism.

ontology_before: A Greek war galley directly inherited from Classical Monumental Construction while its defining multi-level manual propulsion was omitted.
ontology_after: The trireme directly requires manual boat propulsion, while unrelated monumental building practice is absent from its ship-technology boundary.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-greek-trireme-boundary: basic_boat_propulsion is required and construction is absent.
would_reject_if: The target is rescoped from an oared war galley to a monumental civic construction program whose manufacturing scale is the technology being modeled.
short_reason: The trireme is structurally defined by its three-level oar system, not by generic stone-and-civic construction.
